title: Streaming and Async Tool Workflows — Check your understanding

questions.question: A streaming response begins delivering text to the user, then mid-stream a tool_use block starts arriving. What must you do before executing the tool?
options: Execute the tool immediately using the partial tool_use data received so far
Pause text streaming to the user, wait for the complete tool_use block, then execute
Buffer the complete tool_use block before executing — tool calls cannot be executed on partial input, as the arguments may be incomplete
Cancel the stream and restart without streaming enabled
questions.explanation: Tool arguments arrive as partial JSON in input_json_delta events during streaming. A partial argument object is not valid JSON and cannot be executed. You must buffer all input_json_delta events for a given tool call until the content_block_stop event arrives, then parse and execute the complete arguments. Text deltas from earlier in the stream can be shown to the user immediately — only the tool_use block needs to be buffered.

questions.question: A tool starts a background data export job and returns a job_id immediately. The export takes 2–5 minutes to complete. How should the model handle this in the agentic loop?
options: The tool should block for up to 5 minutes and return when complete
The model receives the job_id as a tool result, then calls a check_job_status tool in subsequent loop iterations to poll for completion
The model cannot handle async tools — all tools must complete synchronously
The frontend must poll directly; the model is not involved after triggering the job
questions.explanation: Blocking for minutes in a tool call would exceed HTTP timeouts, block other sessions, and provide no feedback to the user. The correct pattern is: the tool returns immediately with a job_id, and a separate check_job_status tool allows the model to poll in subsequent iterations. The agentic loop naturally handles this — each check_job_status call is a new iteration. The model description in the tool schema ('Returns job_id immediately. Use check_job_status to poll for completion.') tells the model exactly how to proceed.

questions.question: You are building a web interface that streams the agent's response to the browser. Which protocol is standard for server-to-browser one-way streaming?
options: WebSocket — required for any real-time browser communication
Long polling — the browser repeatedly requests updates
Server-sent events (SSE) — a standard HTTP mechanism for pushing a stream of text events from server to browser
gRPC — the most efficient streaming protocol available
questions.explanation: Server-sent events (SSE) are the standard protocol for this use case: the browser opens a single HTTP connection and the server pushes `data:` events as they become available. SSE is supported natively in all modern browsers via the EventSource API, works through most proxies and CDNs, and requires no special server infrastructure. WebSocket provides bidirectional streaming (not needed here) and is more complex to deploy. Long polling adds unnecessary overhead.

questions.question: An async job is started by the model but the user navigates away. The job continues running on the remote service, consuming its resources. What pattern prevents orphaned jobs?
options: Set a server-side timeout on the job — jobs that exceed it are cancelled automatically
Implement cancellation: when the user session ends, call the job's cancel endpoint and confirm cancellation before releasing session resources
Orphaned jobs are unavoidable — remote services are responsible for their own cleanup
Use synchronous tools only to avoid the problem
questions.explanation: Explicit cancellation is the correct primary pattern: when the user session ends, call the job's cancel endpoint so the downstream service stops consuming resources and stops billing for work that will never be used. Server-side timeouts (option A) are a useful safety net for when cancellation fails or is not implemented, but they are not a substitute — a 5-minute timeout still wastes 5 minutes of resources per orphaned job. Relying on timeouts as the primary mechanism is reactive; explicit cancellation is proactive.

questions.question: A streaming response has been running for 25 seconds and the user's connection drops mid-stream. On reconnect, the user wants to resume where they left off. What is the limitation of SSE in this scenario?
options: SSE does not support reconnection at all
SSE supports reconnection via the Last-Event-ID header, but only if the server implements event IDs and buffers recent events — without this, the stream restarts from the beginning
SSE automatically buffers the full stream on the server for reconnection
SSE reconnection is handled by the browser without any server support needed
questions.explanation: The SSE specification includes a Last-Event-ID mechanism: the browser sends the last received event ID on reconnect, and the server can use this to resume from that point. However, this requires the server to assign event IDs, buffer recent events, and implement resume logic. Without server-side support, reconnection restarts the stream from the beginning. For LLM streaming, the practical approach is usually to replay the full response from the start on reconnect, since generation costs are relatively low and buffering full responses adds complexity.
